items.q: Why are Group 2 carbonates more thermally stable down the group?
items.correct: Larger cations have lower charge density, polarise the carbonate ion less and so weaken its C–O bonds less.
items.misconception: They are more stable because bigger atoms form stronger ionic bonds with the carbonate.
items.random_wrong: They are more stable simply because heavier compounds always need more heat.
items.why: Students reach for 'stronger bonds' by default; the real driver is decreasing polarisation of the carbonate anion by the cation.

items.q: Does the flame test colour come from the metal burning?
items.correct: No — heat promotes electrons to higher energy levels, and the colour is light emitted as they drop back down.
items.misconception: The flame colour appears because the metal is combusting in the flame.
items.random_wrong: The colour comes from the chloride ions in the sample.
items.why: Students link flames with combustion; the test actually demonstrates electronic transitions, not a reaction with oxygen.

items.q: Why is the barium chloride solution acidified in the sulfate test?
items.correct: The acid removes carbonate and sulfite ions, which would otherwise also give white precipitates and a false positive.
items.misconception: The acid is needed to dissolve the barium sulfate so it can be observed.
items.random_wrong: The acid makes the solution conduct electricity better.
items.why: Students rarely consider interfering ions; barium sulfate is insoluble in acid, which is exactly why the test still works.

items.q: Which Group 2 carbonate decomposes at the lowest temperature?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: MgCO₃. “BaCO₃” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Going down Group 2, the solubility of the sulfates:
items.why: Correct answer: Decreases. “Increases” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The standard test for sulfate ions in solution uses:
items.why: It's Acidified barium chloride. “Acidified silver nitrate” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The flame colour given by sodium compounds is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Yellow-orange. “Lilac” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Calculate the mass of CO₂ released when 10.0 g of CaCO₃ (Mr = 100.1) decomposes completely (Mr of CO₂ = 44.0).
items.why: Exact answer: 4.4 g. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
